posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
With the World in Chaos and International Tensions running high, one man heads to our Nation’s Capitol…

No, not that man. Another man. This guy. The plot remains interesting, though. A world Class Violinist, a Bistro in the murky underbelly of Wisconsin Avenue, a beauty from a far-off locale, the world most definitely in peril, and two high-class Burgundies.

1998 François Raveneau Chablis 1er Cru Butteaux

Is this World Class Chardonnay? Most Definitely. Is this Chablis? Supposedly . This isn’t Grass and Chalk. This is the high-quality butter from that Grass. Flash-decanted and then enjoyed over a few hours. In the opening hour, the butter content is simply immense. Although a '98, this doesn’t taste much over 10 years old, but what those extra years are providing is a smooth, very necessary integration of oak. What else does it taste like? Frankly, this tastes expensive, in the opulent, intense, decadent sense of the phrase. But can that decadence evolve?

Fortunately, we had access to crispy, lightly-buttered Soft-shell crabs with a few turns of cracked black pepper. The interplay of flavors builds & builds. A perfect match, and maybe an essential one to really dig into this wine’s layers. Even more fortunately, time is on our side… we set aside the final 3 glasses worth and return to it at room temp after the mains. Too many great wines are rushed through. With time, these final glasses are superb. There is white pepper on the finish, and the building of back-end perfumes. With the finish so beautifully rounded out and the wine relaxed and expressive, this is a treasure beyond the opening showiness.

It is a beautiful bottle of wine. If given the time, it will be more than one bottle for you, and will hopefully give you the Joy of a full story at the end of the evening.

2006 Jacky Truchot Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Les Sorbes

Enter the seductive stranger…

I am preternaturally very afraid of drinking Burgundy too young. It’s a real fear of mine. Fear that the primary fruit (even of high quality and sensual bouquet) will over-shadow the mid-palate and the finish. Poker-faced, when a wine like this is initially served, my brain is immediately on edge with so much primary fruit. Will this wine be more than a show-pony of a red silk dress with a high slit up the side? How will it handle its (relative) youth?

This is a High-Fashion model of a wine. Voluptuous, strawberry velvet on the front-end a nose that just radiates glass after glass. Unlike the rich Ravaneau, I’m not entirely sure the food brings out perfection. The matches were strong (particularly a course of sweatbreads and beautiful mushrooms, but also a duck leg confit, and on my side of the table the meat juices from a Veal chop in a fun, succulent interaction). And time rounds out this wine, too.

But its takeaway for me remains that sheer opulence of fruit. It’s achingly, embarrassingly sexy in its nose. It is open, it is expressive, and it remains a front-end beauty all night. I think for some Burgundy seekers, this wine would be an immediate star, so high quality and so seductive is the fruit.

I’ve probably seen too many movies, but my first instinct is to also be suspicious of so much velvet beauty. Burgundy can be a Rorschach Test. I worry about the sleek, overtly stunning beautiful person walking through the door… And yet, in the end, there is nothing to worry about. This really is a treasure of a Burgundy. Its sleek, velvet red fruits don’t get stuck in the mire of unbalance. There is no phenolic heat on the finish. The meal, if anything, only enhances its youthful sweetness.

This is a style of wine I drink so infrequently. Generally, if people give me choices from their cellar, I often choose older selections, most curious to see what combinations of age and integration ensue. So I am indebted to my friends for pulling a wine that startles me in its freshness. I took 90 minutes with this wine, diving into its bouquet again and again.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
well written notes on a couple of great wines
Cheers

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Great notes and great wines. Love the 2006 les Sorbes. But the “murky underbelly of Wisconsin Avenue”??? Look at housing prices near Wisconsin Ave from Georgetown until it becomes Rockville Pike and beyond. There is no such thing. The one murky underbelly I think of is a college campus in Durham full of a rat.

posts.name: Michael Warner
posts.cooked: I thought 2005 was Jacky Truchot’s final vintage.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Great notes and great wines. Love the 2006 les Sorbes. But the “murky underbelly of Wisconsin Avenue”??? Look at housing prices near Wisconsin Ave from Georgetown until it becomes Rockville Pike and beyond. There is no such thing. The one murky underbelly I think of is a college campus in Durham full of a rat.

First off, I shall have you know that Duke Gardens now has a delicate and unique lotus pond . Which in the hot months I now enjoy weekly, frequently with a sublime Ethiopian medium roast. West campus grows more and more gated-off and ivory towerish (to my great annoyance) but Humble East (Old Trinity) and Duke Gardens flourish. Come down, we’ll go for a picnic… I mean, I’d even stroll around the Gimghoul loop with you just to let you know I appreciate lovely areas on all points of the Triangle, Howard.

Secondly, while we must demand 100% honesty from one another on the wines themselves, one may indulge in poetic license in the surrounding story elements… And does a neighborhood, even a gentrified one, ever entirely lose its corners of mystery?

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
I thought 2005 was Jacky Truchot’s final vintage.

The DC Burgundian Intelligentsia can chime in on this point with more accuracy and story elements, but apparently The Great Man kept a few corners of this site for personal projects. Less than a half-dozen barrels of this vintage, I am told. With one of those barrels journeying across the Atlantic.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Thank you for reading, Marcus & Hank! These were fun wines to think about…

And, Marcus, I am automatically jealous at this point in my life of any person living in New Zealand, so you have my admiration and envy .

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Never thought of Truchot as “high fashion.”

This particular bottle is a gorgeous silk fabric of a wine right now .

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Never thought of Truchot as “high fashion.”

This particular bottle is a gorgeous silk fabric of a wine right now .

Now I need to go dig one out!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Great notes and great wines. Love the 2006 les Sorbes. But the “murky underbelly of Wisconsin Avenue”??? Look at housing prices near Wisconsin Ave from Georgetown until it becomes Rockville Pike and beyond. There is no such thing. The one murky underbelly I think of is a college campus in Durham full of a rat.

First off, I shall have you know that Duke Gardens now has a delicate and unique lotus pond . Which in the hot months I now enjoy weekly, frequently with a sublime Ethiopian medium roast. West campus grows more and more gated-off and ivory towerish (to my great annoyance) but Humble East (Old Trinity) and Duke Gardens flourish. Come down, we’ll go for a picnic… I mean, I’d even stroll around the Gimghoul loop with you just to let you know I appreciate lovely areas on all points of the Triangle, Howard.

Secondly, while we must demand 100% honesty from one another on the wines themselves, one may indulge in poetic license in the surrounding story elements… And does a neighborhood, even a gentrified one, ever entirely lose its corners of mystery?

As I went to college 9 miles away from there, I will never say anything nice about the place- you know, poetic license and all.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I thought 2005 was Jacky Truchot’s final vintage.

The DC Burgundian Intelligentsia can chime in on this point with more accuracy and story elements, but apparently The Great Man kept a few corners of this site for personal projects. Less than a half-dozen barrels of this vintage, I am told. With one of those barrels journeying across the Atlantic.

The Truchot plot in Les Sorbes is pretty much the backyard of a house the Truchots own or owned in MSD. As such, it was not sold with the rest of their vineyards. Wine continued to be made from this small plot but was only sold commercially as I understand it from the 2006 vintage. The 2006, as Nick said, is quite good.

Bistro Burgundies.jpg

No notes on the Selbach?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Nice Nick! Those two bottles are very special. A rare treat. Glad all is well with you.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Nice Nick! Those two bottles are very special. A rare treat. Glad all is well with you.

Thank you, my friend . I owe you an email with a few updates, which I shall aspire to write later this evening, but yes, all really is quite well. Both within the wine community and without.

We missed you everywhere, but especially in Toronto a few weeks back.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
No notes on the Selbach?

Oh, I do .

The goal is to open a little Riesling later in the weekend with someone and then maybe group those two together, thematically… as I was writing up the Burgs, I felt like a screenwriter with one Act too many.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Enjoyed these notes very much, Nick!

Truchot produced this cuvée under his name through 2017, and in a last bottling that doesn’t carry his name (or anyone’s) or the vineyard’s in 2018.

In 2019, he sold the vineyard, frustrated that the close friends he’d been sharing the wine with were all flipping it instead of drinking it.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Enjoyed these notes very much, Nick!

Truchot produced this cuvée under his name through 2017, and in a last bottling that doesn’t carry his name (or anyone’s) or the vineyard’s in 2018.

In 2019, he sold the vineyard, frustrated that the close friends he’d been sharing the wine with were all flipping it instead of drinking it.

Aw, thank you, my friend . I am so glad that so many awesome folks were able to read these.

Thanks for the additional update as well… if a bit Melancholy.
